title: Bacterial Meningitis
oneLiner: Life-threatening infection of the meninges; predominantly community-acquired (Neisseria meningitidis, Streptococcus pneumoniae); kills within hours if antibiotics are delayed.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Meningococcal sepsis with fulminant purpura:** non-blanching petechial/purpuric rash that spreads rapidly; this is **not** a routine case — give IV antibiotics immediately without LP or imaging.
- **Raised intracranial pressure / impending coning:** GCS < 13 + Cushing’s reflex (bradycardia, hypertension, irregular breathing) or unilateral dilated pupil; this is **not** a routine case — call senior, start mannitol / hyperventilation, do NOT do an LP without CT.
sections.Presentation:
- Classic triad (but **all three present in < 50%** of adults): fever, neck stiffness, altered mental state
- **Other cardinal symptoms:** severe headache, photophobia, nausea/vomiting
- **Exam findings:**
  - Neck stiffness (Kernig’s sign — less reliable)
  - Non-blanching petechial or purpuric rash (meningococcus)
  - Brudzinski’s sign (passive neck flexion → hips/knees flex)
- In elderly / immunocompromised: may only have confusion, fever, or no neck stiffness
- Seizures / focal neurology in severe cases
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Viral meningitis:** less toxic, no rash, often prior viral prodrome; CSF: lymphocytic, normal glucose
- **Encephalitis (e.g. HSV):** altered behaviour, focal seizures, temporal lobe signs; CSF PCR for HSV
- **Subarachnoid haemorrhage:** sudden “thunderclap” headache, neck stiffness, no fever; CT head shows blood
- **Brain abscess:** progressive focal neurology, signs of mass effect, sinusitis/otitis source; CT/MRI shows ring-enhancing lesion
- **Cerebral malaria (if travel history):** fever, coma; blood film for parasites
- **Drug-induced aseptic meningitis (e.g. NSAIDs, IVIG):** no rash, CSF sterile, recent drug history
- **Splitter:** petechial/purpuric rash → bacterial (meningococcal) until proven otherwise
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Document GCS, vital signs, rash mapping
  - Blood glucose (BM) to differentiate hypoglycaemia
  - Lumbar puncture (LP) — **do not delay antibiotics for LP**; perform once stable and no CT contraindications (GCS < 13, papilloedema, focal neurology, immunocompromised, recent seizure, coagulopathy)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, clotting, lactate
  - Blood culture (x2) — before antibiotics if possible but never delay treatment
  - Meningococcal / pneumococcal PCR (EDTA blood)
- **Imaging:**
  - CT head: indicated before LP if one of the above concerns; otherwise not mandatory
  - MRI brain: if encephalitis or abscess suspected, or if no improvement
- **Special tests:**
  - CSF: opening pressure, WBC + differential, protein, glucose, Gram stain, culture, PCR for meningococcus/pneumococcus/Listeria/HSV
  - CSF lactate > 3.5 mmol/L suggests bacterial over viral
- **Diagnostic criteria:** NICE Clinical Guideline [CG102]: empirical treatment for suspected bacterial meningitis should begin immediately on suspicion, without waiting for LP results
- **Never-miss test:** Blood cultures + LP (if safe) — omission of LP after antibiotics reduces diagnostic yield; omission of blood cultures loses organism identification
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - IV/IO antibiotics: Ceftriaxone 2 g stat (or Cefotaxime 2 g) — **give within 60 minutes of suspicion**
  - If suspected meningococcus: also notify infection control / public health immediately
  - IV Dexamethasone 10 mg (adults) — before or with first antibiotic dose; continue 10 mg IV q6h x 4 days
  - Resuscitate: IV fluids (0.9% saline 500 mL bolus if hypotensive), oxygen to target SpO2 > 94%
  - Manage raised ICP: head up 30°, mannitol 0.25-1 g/kg IV if coning imminent
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue ceftriaxone 2 g IV q12h (or according to local sensitivities)
  - If penicillin-resistant pneumococcus suspected: add vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV q12h [check local policy]
  - If Listeria suspected (elderly, immunocompromised, pregnancy): add ampicillin 2 g IV q4h
  - Monitor GCS, NEWS2, urine output, input/output chart
  - Repeat LP if no improvement after 48h (to check sterilisation)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete 7–14 days IV (meningococcus 7d, pneumococcus 10–14d, Listeria 21d)
  - Determine need for prophylactic antibiotics for close contacts (rifampicin, ciprofloxacin, or ceftriaxone)
  - Arrange audiology assessment before discharge (sensorineural hearing loss common)
  - Vaccination: meningococcal conjugate + pneumococcal post-discharge (if serotype known)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% saline with 5% dextrose (maintenance e.g. 1 L per 8 hours, adjust for patient)
  - VTE prophylaxis: prophylactic LMWH if no coagulopathy (ensuring no LP with coagulopathy)
  - Diet: NBM until swallow assessment if GCS reduced; otherwise normal
  - Observations: NEWS2 minimum q1h initially, then q4h once stable
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line:**
  - Ceftriaxone 2 g IV q12h (adults); 2 g IV q24h if aged > 65 or eGFR < 20 [check local policy for paediatrics]
  - Dexamethasone 10 mg IV q6h x 4 days (start before/with first dose of antibiotics)
- **Alternatives:**
  - Cefotaxime 2 g IV q6h (if ceftriaxone contraindicated)
  - Chloramphenicol 1 g IV q6h (severe penicillin allergy) [check local policy]
  - Vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV q12h (for resistant pneumococcus) — monitor trough levels (keep 15-20 μg/mL)
  - Ampicillin 2 g IV q4h (for Listeria cover)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Ceftriaxone: avoid in neonatal hyperbilirubinaemia; may cause biliary sludging (rare)
  - Dexamethasone: may mask fever; avoid in septic shock unless adrenal insufficiency suspected
  - Vancomycin: nephrotoxic, requires trough level monitoring
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Ceftriaxone: no dose adjustment in renal impairment; caution in severe hepatic/renal dual disease
  - Vancomycin: adjust dose per eGFR; check trough levels pre-4th dose
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Delaying antibiotics to check allergy history or to perform LP — give IV ceftriaxone stat regardless; for patients with anaphylaxis to penicillins/cephalosporins, use chloramphenicol [check local policy] — but never wait
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Septic shock / DIC / purpura fulminans
  - Seizures (focal or generalised)
  - Hydrocephalus (communicating or obstructive)
  - Cerebral oedema / herniation
  - Subdural empyema, brain abscess
- **Chronic:**
  - Sensorineural hearing loss (most common long-term)
  - Cognitive deficits, epilepsy, hydrocephalus requiring shunt
  - Limb ischaemia / amputation (meningococcal)
  - Arthralgia / arthritis (post-meningococcal)
sections.Prognosis:
- Mortality: ~10-15% overall; 20-30% in pneumococcal; <5% in meningococcal with early treatment
- Neurological sequelae in ~30-50% of survivors (hearing loss, cognitive impairment)
- Worse prognosis: extremes of age, low GCS, seizures, delay in antibiotics, pneumococcal aetiology
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Give dexamethasone 10 mg IV immediately before or WITH the first dose of antibiotics** — reduces hearing loss and neurological sequelae in pneumococcal meningitis
- **Do LP as soon as safe** — but only after ruling out raised ICP; if unstable, treat empirically and do LP after stabilisation
- **Notify public health / infection control early** — to arrange prophylactic antibiotics for close contacts (rifampicin 600 mg PO q12h x 2 days; or ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO single dose; or ceftriaxone 250 mg IM single dose)
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Waiting for LP before starting antibiotics** — antibiotics started even minutes earlier reduce mortality; you can still culture bacteria from blood and CSF for hours after first dose
- **Not considering Listeria in at-risk patients** — elderly, pregnant, immunocompromised, > 50 years: add ampicillin to empirical ceftriaxone
- **Forgetting to reassise contact prophylaxis** — after patient is confirmed meningococcal, ensure household contacts, close friends, healthcare workers with substantial exposure are offered prophylaxis
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Worsening headache, new petechial/purpuric rash, fever, confusion, stiff neck, seizure, or hearing loss
- **Follow-up:** 2-week outpatient clinic review (neurology / infectious diseases), audiology appointment within 4 weeks, general practice for vaccination (meningococcal ACWY + B, pneumococcal)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (ST3+ / consultant) immediately if:**
  - GCS drops by ≥ 2 points or GCS < 13
  - New focal neurology or seizure
  - Signs of raised ICP (Cushing’s reflex, pupil changes)
  - Respiratory failure / hypotension not responding to fluids
  - Purpura fulminans (rapidly spreading necrotic rash)
- **ICU referral criteria:**
  - GCS < 8 or deteriorating
  - Refractory shock (lactate > 4, requiring vasopressors)
  - Seizures not controlled, or status epilepticus
  - Need for invasive mechanical ventilation
  - NEWS2 ≥ 7 or persistent NEWS2 ≥ 5 despite treatment

rawMarkdown:
## Bacterial Meningitis
**One-liner:** Life-threatening infection of the meninges; predominantly community-acquired (Neisseria meningitidis, Streptococcus pneumoniae); kills within hours if antibiotics are delayed.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Meningococcal sepsis with fulminant purpura:** non-blanching petechial/purpuric rash that spreads rapidly; this is **not** a routine case — give IV antibiotics immediately without LP or imaging.
- **Raised intracranial pressure / impending coning:** GCS < 13 + Cushing’s reflex (bradycardia, hypertension, irregular breathing) or unilateral dilated pupil; this is **not** a routine case — call senior, start mannitol / hyperventilation, do NOT do an LP without CT.

### Presentation
- Classic triad (but **all three present in < 50%** of adults): fever, neck stiffness, altered mental state
- **Other cardinal symptoms:** severe headache, photophobia, nausea/vomiting
- **Exam findings:**
  - Neck stiffness (Kernig’s sign — less reliable)
  - Non-blanching petechial or purpuric rash (meningococcus)
  - Brudzinski’s sign (passive neck flexion → hips/knees flex)
- In elderly / immunocompromised: may only have confusion, fever, or no neck stiffness
- Seizures / focal neurology in severe cases

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Viral meningitis:** less toxic, no rash, often prior viral prodrome; CSF: lymphocytic, normal glucose
- **Encephalitis (e.g. HSV):** altered behaviour, focal seizures, temporal lobe signs; CSF PCR for HSV
- **Subarachnoid haemorrhage:** sudden “thunderclap” headache, neck stiffness, no fever; CT head shows blood
- **Brain abscess:** progressive focal neurology, signs of mass effect, sinusitis/otitis source; CT/MRI shows ring-enhancing lesion
- **Cerebral malaria (if travel history):** fever, coma; blood film for parasites
- **Drug-induced aseptic meningitis (e.g. NSAIDs, IVIG):** no rash, CSF sterile, recent drug history
- **Splitter:** petechial/purpuric rash → bacterial (meningococcal) until proven otherwise

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Document GCS, vital signs, rash mapping
  - Blood glucose (BM) to differentiate hypoglycaemia
  - Lumbar puncture (LP) — **do not delay antibiotics for LP**; perform once stable and no CT contraindications (GCS < 13, papilloedema, focal neurology, immunocompromised, recent seizure, coagulopathy)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, clotting, lactate
  - Blood culture (x2) — before antibiotics if possible but never delay treatment
  - Meningococcal / pneumococcal PCR (EDTA blood)
- **Imaging:**
  - CT head: indicated before LP if one of the above concerns; otherwise not mandatory
  - MRI brain: if encephalitis or abscess suspected, or if no improvement
- **Special tests:**
  - CSF: opening pressure, WBC + differential, protein, glucose, Gram stain, culture, PCR for meningococcus/pneumococcus/Listeria/HSV
  - CSF lactate > 3.5 mmol/L suggests bacterial over viral
- **Diagnostic criteria:** NICE Clinical Guideline [CG102]: empirical treatment for suspected bacterial meningitis should begin immediately on suspicion, without waiting for LP results
- **Never-miss test:** Blood cultures + LP (if safe) — omission of LP after antibiotics reduces diagnostic yield; omission of blood cultures loses organism identification

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - IV/IO antibiotics: Ceftriaxone 2 g stat (or Cefotaxime 2 g) — **give within 60 minutes of suspicion**
  - If suspected meningococcus: also notify infection control / public health immediately
  - IV Dexamethasone 10 mg (adults) — before or with first antibiotic dose; continue 10 mg IV q6h x 4 days
  - Resuscitate: IV fluids (0.9% saline 500 mL bolus if hypotensive), oxygen to target SpO2 > 94%
  - Manage raised ICP: head up 30°, mannitol 0.25-1 g/kg IV if coning imminent
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue ceftriaxone 2 g IV q12h (or according to local sensitivities)
  - If penicillin-resistant pneumococcus suspected: add vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV q12h [check local policy]
  - If Listeria suspected (elderly, immunocompromised, pregnancy): add ampicillin 2 g IV q4h
  - Monitor GCS, NEWS2, urine output, input/output chart
  - Repeat LP if no improvement after 48h (to check sterilisation)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete 7–14 days IV (meningococcus 7d, pneumococcus 10–14d, Listeria 21d)
  - Determine need for prophylactic antibiotics for close contacts (rifampicin, ciprofloxacin, or ceftriaxone)
  - Arrange audiology assessment before discharge (sensorineural hearing loss common)
  - Vaccination: meningococcal conjugate + pneumococcal post-discharge (if serotype known)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% saline with 5% dextrose (maintenance e.g. 1 L per 8 hours, adjust for patient)
  - VTE prophylaxis: prophylactic LMWH if no coagulopathy (ensuring no LP with coagulopathy)
  - Diet: NBM until swallow assessment if GCS reduced; otherwise normal
  - Observations: NEWS2 minimum q1h initially, then q4h once stable

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line:**
  - Ceftriaxone 2 g IV q12h (adults); 2 g IV q24h if aged > 65 or eGFR < 20 [check local policy for paediatrics]
  - Dexamethasone 10 mg IV q6h x 4 days (start before/with first dose of antibiotics)
- **Alternatives:**
  - Cefotaxime 2 g IV q6h (if ceftriaxone contraindicated)
  - Chloramphenicol 1 g IV q6h (severe penicillin allergy) [check local policy]
  - Vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV q12h (for resistant pneumococcus) — monitor trough levels (keep 15-20 μg/mL)
  - Ampicillin 2 g IV q4h (for Listeria cover)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Ceftriaxone: avoid in neonatal hyperbilirubinaemia; may cause biliary sludging (rare)
  - Dexamethasone: may mask fever; avoid in septic shock unless adrenal insufficiency suspected
  - Vancomycin: nephrotoxic, requires trough level monitoring
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Ceftriaxone: no dose adjustment in renal impairment; caution in severe hepatic/renal dual disease
  - Vancomycin: adjust dose per eGFR; check trough levels pre-4th dose
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Delaying antibiotics to check allergy history or to perform LP — give IV ceftriaxone stat regardless; for patients with anaphylaxis to penicillins/cephalosporins, use chloramphenicol [check local policy] — but never wait

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Septic shock / DIC / purpura fulminans
  - Seizures (focal or generalised)
  - Hydrocephalus (communicating or obstructive)
  - Cerebral oedema / herniation
  - Subdural empyema, brain abscess
- **Chronic:**
  - Sensorineural hearing loss (most common long-term)
  - Cognitive deficits, epilepsy, hydrocephalus requiring shunt
  - Limb ischaemia / amputation (meningococcal)
  - Arthralgia / arthritis (post-meningococcal)

### Prognosis
- Mortality: ~10-15% overall; 20-30% in pneumococcal; <5% in meningococcal with early treatment
- Neurological sequelae in ~30-50% of survivors (hearing loss, cognitive impairment)
- Worse prognosis: extremes of age, low GCS, seizures, delay in antibiotics, pneumococcal aetiology

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Give dexamethasone 10 mg IV immediately before or WITH the first dose of antibiotics** — reduces hearing loss and neurological sequelae in pneumococcal meningitis
- **Do LP as soon as safe** — but only after ruling out raised ICP; if unstable, treat empirically and do LP after stabilisation
- **Notify public health / infection control early** — to arrange prophylactic antibiotics for close contacts (rifampicin 600 mg PO q12h x 2 days; or ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO single dose; or ceftriaxone 250 mg IM single dose)

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Waiting for LP before starting antibiotics** — antibiotics started even minutes earlier reduce mortality; you can still culture bacteria from blood and CSF for hours after first dose
- **Not considering Listeria in at-risk patients** — elderly, pregnant, immunocompromised, > 50 years: add ampicillin to empirical ceftriaxone
- **Forgetting to reassise contact prophylaxis** — after patient is confirmed meningococcal, ensure household contacts, close friends, healthcare workers with substantial exposure are offered prophylaxis

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Worsening headache, new petechial/purpuric rash, fever, confusion, stiff neck, seizure, or hearing loss
- **Follow-up:** 2-week outpatient clinic review (neurology / infectious diseases), audiology appointment within 4 weeks, general practice for vaccination (meningococcal ACWY + B, pneumococcal)

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (ST3+ / consultant) immediately if:**
  - GCS drops by ≥ 2 points or GCS < 13
  - New focal neurology or seizure
  - Signs of raised ICP (Cushing’s reflex, pupil changes)
  - Respiratory failure / hypotension not responding to fluids
  - Purpura fulminans (rapidly spreading necrotic rash)
- **ICU referral criteria:**
  - GCS < 8 or deteriorating
  - Refractory shock (lactate > 4, requiring vasopressors)
  - Seizures not controlled, or status epilepticus
  - Need for invasive mechanical ventilation
  - NEWS2 ≥ 7 or persistent NEWS2 ≥ 5 despite treatment
